original_abstract: This article provides a critical assessment of Keith Ward’s theological framework and its interpretation of reality, suggesting that his work aligns with what may be termed "soft panentheism." By examining Ward’s attempts to reconcile materialist scientific worldviews with spiritual interpretations, the study argues that he utilizes panentheistic criteria—specifically a bipolar construct of God—to bridge the gap between divine transcendence and immanence. Using a general reformational theological perspective as a point of departure, the author explores the contemporary rise of panentheism and analyzes its increasing appeal among certain Christian circles. The paper investigates how Ward’s particular approach to revelation and "God-experience" shifts the focus from traditional propositional theology to a dynamic, process-oriented understanding of deity. Finally, the research offers recommendations for those tempted by generic Christian panentheism, cautioning against the replacement of classical theism with a model that may compromise the ontological self-sufficiency of God as traditionally understood in the Christian faith.
humanized_abstract: From my assessment this theory is better understood as soft panentheism, as I will explain and assess below, incorporating in particular Ward’s attempt to integrate scientific materialism into a theistic framework utilising a set of criteria appropriate to the category of panentheism referred to by the bipolar formula God in more than one sense. In the broader context of the reformational tradition the category of panentheism together with its soft variant is a trend in present-day theism to which one discerns not inconsiderable developments amongst some branches of Christian theology. Building on his presentation of God's 'divine realm', and employing the theory of "God-experience" instead of the doctrine of revelation according to traditional Protestant dogmatics, Ward turns the Christian dogmatic discipline on its head as he integrates within it his philosophical theistic process theory of God. An urgent word of warning however will be called for by anyone, tempted by this general Christian panentheism.
